On June 24, 1994, The Washington Times reported that "Croatia has become a major transit point for covert Iranian arms shipments to Bosnia with the tacit approval of the Clinton Administration."

A Versatile Low-Fat Protein? There's Always Canned Tuna
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SOME years ago, a food writer wrote that he was astonished about a lunch Julia Child served him that included a salade nicoise topped with canned tuna. He forgot, of course, that the classic version of the dish is supposed to have canned tuna. 

     For most of us, canned tuna is the pantry item of last resort, but Andy Black, the author of "A Can of Tuna" (Prism Press, 1995), calls it "the Cinderella of foodstuffs." It is a convenient low-fat protein that can be quickly transformed into an array of attractive dishes, and for about half the price of fresh tuna.

At the Nation's Table: Austin, Tex.;Cookies and Milk Start a Career
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Freshly baked cookies with a glass of milk is comfort food hard to come by these days for those without an obliging mom or other willing household baker. To fill that void and inaugurate a career as an entrepreneur, Paul Rodriguez has started Mount Bushishnu, a cookies-and-milk delivery service. 

     Mr. Rodriguez, 21, dropped out of Amherst College after a year (he said Massachusetts was too cold). He was working as a bicycle messenger at home in Texas when the idea struck him: offer University of Texas students a deliverable alternative to pizza when hunger attacks in evening study hours. He said he named his company after a favorite character in a science fiction television program (he added Mount "for a sense of permanence").

At the Nation's Table: Savannah, Ga.;A Chef Promotes Market Cuisine
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When asked what excited him most about creating a new menu for Bistro Savannah, a seven-year-old restaurant in what Margaret Mitchell called "that gently mannered city by the sea in Georgia," the new chef, Christopher Nason, answered with what has become his culinary mantra: "fresh market cuisine." Appointed chef of the bistro a month ago, Mr. Nason said his goal was to serve sophisticated, healthy food, using organically grown local and regional produce and the herbs and seafood that are available daily. 

     For example, this month Mr. Nason is cooking with chanterelles grown at a nearby farm, while the rainwater Madeira he roasts them in originated in Savannah. The red snapper he tosses into a bouillabaisse was hauled in that morning by a fishing boat owned by the restaurant. Mr. Nason is also cultivating heirloom herbs and vegetables that flourished in Savannah before the Civil War.
